turns.content: I've been reading about upcasting in Rust where you can coerce a subtype reference to a supertrait, but I still don't get exactly how &dyn SubTrait becomes &dyn SuperTrait and why my code is failing.

The key requirement is that the supertrait must be a single base trait. When you have &dyn MyTrait which implements Log, you can only coerce to &dyn Log if there are no other direct supertraits for MyTrait besides Log itself.
Wait, I thought the vtable layout allowed multiple inheritance upcasting automatically? Isn't that how polymorphism works in most languages?

Rust's vtable is different; it only supports a single base trait for upcasting to work correctly. If MyTrait has multiple direct supertraits, the compiler cannot determine which one you want to coerce to.
So I can't use &dyn MyTrait as &dyn Log if MyTrait also extends something else like Clone or Debug?
Exactly. If MyTrait implements both Log and Clone, you cannot coerce it to &dyn Log because there's ambiguity in the vtable layout.
That makes sense, but what if I explicitly implement only Log on MyTrait? Can I then safely cast?
Yes, as long as MyTrait's trait bounds don't include other supertraits beyond Log. The compiler will generate the correct vtable pointer for &dyn Log.
And this is only available in stable Rust since 1.76, right?
Correct, this feature stabilized in Rust 1.76. Before that you had to use trait objects with explicit type erasure instead of automatic upcasting.
So for logging where I want a generic interface but need to pass down to a specific logger, this is perfect?
Perfect. You can define a trait Log with a method like log_message, then have MyTrait implement only that trait so &dyn MyTrait coerces to &dyn Log.

I'm porting a C++ logging system to Rust and I keep hitting this wall: my &dyn MyLog trait objects don't seem to coerce up to &dyn Loggable when I have multiple supertraits. The docs say it's stable in 1.76, but my compiler is screaming about invalid vtable layouts.

You've hit the exact limitation of this feature. Trait upcasting from a concrete trait like MyLog to an abstract one only works if that supertrait is a single base; if Loggable itself has multiple direct supertraits, the vtable layout breaks because there's no way to map the single pointer to multiple inheritance points. The compiler is right to reject it in this scenario.
So I can't just rely on automatic coercion? Does that mean I have to manually implement Loggable for every MyLog implementation, or is there a pattern where vtable layout actually helps?

The vtable layout only helps when the supertrait has a single base. If you need upcasting in a multi-inheritance hierarchy, you can't rely on automatic coercion; you must manually implement Loggable for each concrete MyLog implementation or refactor your design so that one of those bases becomes the sole parent of Loggable.
That sounds painful. I was hoping the vtable trick would let me treat all my logging services uniformly without extra boilerplate.

Unfortunately, in your multi-base scenario, there's no automatic way to make &dyn MyLog coerce to &dyn Loggable; you'll need to either add a single base trait that both share or implement the interface explicitly for each service. The vtable layout is what prevents this coercion when multiple bases are involved.
Got it, so upcasting isn't just about convenience here—it's fundamentally limited by how Rust structures trait objects with multiple parents. I guess my logging abstraction needs a redesign then.

Exactly; the limitation is structural rather than accidental. If you want uniformity without manual implementation, consider consolidating your logging hierarchy into a single base trait and letting MyLog inherit from it, which restores vtable compatibility for upcasting.
That makes sense. One more thing: does this mean I can't use std::any to store heterogeneous logs across different services in a generic way?

Not with &dyn Loggable if your hierarchy has multiple bases; you might need to store them as boxed trait objects or separate crates for each service, since the automatic upcasting path is blocked by the vtable constraints.
